item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really love how the victims are meticulous, and minimize damage and risk of infection. I almost picture them being fine with what they've just done once they let go of the cutter. Also I love the implication that it's actually an improvement (or at least tries to be), like LASIK or something. This is much further from the "disturbing compulsion" cliché than it may seem at first (with the blood and preschool mentions priming you to expect uncreative gratuitous gruesomeness), IMO!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Would this have been improved by a 3000 word backstory about what is done with a collection of thousands of tiny eyelids?

Mhm. You say this as a joke, but there's some good imagery here. The thought of all those eyelids going somewhere. Some poor schmuck opens up a cabinet five miles away and ends up under an avalanche of eyelids.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't quite bring myself to upvote this because it's really lame.

But also it's really simple and effective. It's such a horrible idea, in a good way, it just works fine as it is. I'd also ditch the night vision stuff as people suggested.

Good example of how you can take one thing that really gets to your shiver bones, and make a nice simple SCP out of it. Does what it says on the tin, so to speak.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is so basic and unpleasant that I am genuinely surprised that anyone has downvoted it ;)

Would this have been improved by a 3000 word backstory about what is done with a collection of thousands of tiny eyelids? I say no: thus my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love straightforward and simple things but this just seems to be a little bit a mutilation and nothing else. So it falls more under "gross" than "disturbing". The night-vision thing is kinda cool but it's so simplistic and short it doesn't really cause any intrigue or anything. Really this could just be replaced by a video of a guy cutting off his eyelids and not much would change.

item.title: Blade Removal
item.content:encoded:
If the effect has been shown to be only in the handle, with tests showing that removing the blade has no change to its effect, then shouldn't part of the containment procedures involve removing the blade when not being tested?

If there's no blade, then even if the object is accidentally touched, less harm will come to whoever is holding it, while at the same time, not harming or changing the SCP. It seems like a simple procedure to significantly reduce the danger of the object.

item.title: Brilliant
item.content:encoded: I love it simple straightforward and new!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the kind of article I'd have upvoted in series 1.

I still did, I just would've then too 😉

Approval for such testing from the Ethics Committee has been repeatedly denied.

This is my favorite line because it comes across like the research team is so indignant that nobody's letting them cut people's eyelids off to see if it causes insomnia.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
AAAAAA THIS DOES NOT MAKE ME FEEL COMFORTABLE
AAAAAA upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the reason the reason I upvoted was the footnote. I have a sneaking suspicion that this box cutter may be a little more dangerous than it seems. +1 for subtlety.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm gonna have to novote. While it's pretty well done, it's nothing special. The body horror is mediocre, and there is no semblance of a narrative here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, but is it the top or bottom eyelid, or both?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very little is explained regarding the night vision element or why the eyes don’t simply dry up once the eyelid is removed. Do a person’s eyes stay anomalously wet? Does the night vision result from an anomalous biological process or is unexplainable? It just stretches disbelief that these two elements would be ignored and that so much description would be dedicated to the compulsion effect.

In the end I have to downvote for the above reasons as well as this being another compulsion item, not to mention the bit about the preschool and being covered in blood, which felt a bit cheap.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think this is "haphazardly" choosing to mulitale children. I think it was giving the kids better nightvision. It's just like piercing their ears. It hurts, but it improves your life enough that you'd subject a toddler to it1.

Footnotes
1. Well, I wouldn't, and maybe you wouldn't, but some people are piercing the ears of toddlers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You could use an in-situ photo of a boxcutter rather than one that's floating on a white background (how and why would foundation staff take a photo like this?).

Even though I didn't really like this article very much, I still find this sort of complaint to be pretty unfounded.

How: Light boxes are easy to construct—I've made a number myself out of household materials. It's no stretch of the imagination to think the Foundation would have no difficulty acquiring one.

Why: It provides a clear, detailed view of the object without any background distractions. It also looks appropriately sterile and professional, in keeping with the Foundation's MO.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A generic thing that causes you to mutilate yourself. It's not particularly scary or interesting as a concept but the article's well written enough that I think it sort of works. Great work there.

What got me to -1 here was the word "Preschool".
In my opinion harming children in an article should be handled with care, preferably with some basic justification as to why you've chosen to mutilate children and not adults. Slamming this in so haphazardly, seemingly for cheap shock value, absolutely destroyed any enjoyment I would have gotten out of reading this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's not how that works. The only way that would happen is if the author of 1307 deleted their article, then minmin deleted this and reposted it in the 1307 slot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You could use an in-situ photo of a boxcutter rather than one that's floating on a white background (how and why would foundation staff take a photo like this?).

shit, take a boxcutter from your junk drawer and snap a photo of it on your kitchen counter, then slap a vignette effect on it for that "manila folder secret document" feel

Great writing either way, a nice simple article

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There’s some nice window dressing on this but it’s still just a basic self harm compulsion effect. Having it target children isn’t enough to carry what is otherwise an extremely trite concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Shouldn’t it be obvious that it’s a part of the anomolous properties that it gives you night vision? Cutting your eyelids completely off would normally make it impossible to blink and will make your eyes dry up and go blind, unless I’m missing something here.

Edit: I will upvote this when this is explained, otherwise I’m going to have to novote on this one.

to hell with it, im upvoting because i absolutely love the imagery!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed it. Short, sweet, freaky and now I'm adding Visine to my Amazon list. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We should make some kind of proposal in its own thread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had mixed feelings on the simplicity at first, though I suppose it is a solid body horror skip, so I'll upvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded. Can we replace SCP-1307 with this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: TBH, I actually think that overall this skip is kinda just an better version of 1307.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoy this short and simple article, and I almost felt my eyes hurting reading this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very catching body horror. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The original location it was found in really makes the article. Without it I would definitely give it a -1. Unfortunately, it does seem a little overdone. (Although admittedly, I actually like this better than a lot of the articles it seems similar to.)

item.title: Interesting...
item.content:encoded:
I downvoted the first time I read it thinking it was just another self harm compulsion object.
On closer viewing, this is well written, simple, and mildly disturbing. It works because it hits that magic point of providing enough information to be clear, while hiding enough to let the imagination go wild.
It's a return to the old style of SCPs which didn't need 20 paragraphs and a obscure idea from a philosophy class to be interesting.
The eyelid removal is nearly surgical and raises so many questions as to why the object exists.
The fact that it was recovered at a preschool is truly horrifying.
I like it.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is there something I'm missing here?

I was genuinely weirded out to the point of mean twitching in the real world for reals, but I feel like theres more you could do with this. Unless I'm missing some deeper story here

Novote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The body horror is nice, I just wish it wasn't delivered via the old thing-what-makes-you-hurt-yourself format that we've already seen a bajillion times.

EDIT: Even the preschool hook feels derivative of SCP-1307.

item.title: my brand!
item.content:encoded:
img source - Public Domain. Retrieved from https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Box-cutter.jpg .

review credits to Lily, clubby, Avocadonut, ARD, and TheeSherm
